metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that verifies the Phase 3 and Phase 4a amendments exist (e.g. grep for a model-tier keyword within those sections), so all three named Approach deliverables have a corresponding acceptance check.
evidence: Approach text: 'Add a ## Model tiering operating principle and targeted amendments to Phases 3, 4a, 4d, plus a new ### 4e-bis escalation ladder.'
AC2: 'Phase 4d states a standard-tier default … — checked by reading section 4d.' — no AC covers Phase 3 or 4a amendments.
Prior plan-review cache at .rebar/cache/plan-review/6375-f80b-00ea-4083/1da510fc707353d8cc74.json independently identified this same gap.
findings.finding: The Approach names 'targeted amendments to Phases 3, 4a, 4d' as explicit deliverables, but no Acceptance Criterion verifies that Phases 3 and 4a were actually amended — only Phase 4d is covered (AC2).
findings.location: Approach section ('targeted amendments to Phases 3, 4a, 4d') vs. AC2 (reads only section 4d)
scenarios: An implementer adds the Model tiering section and 4e-bis but omits the Phase 3 and 4a amendments; all five ACs pass, yet the stated scope is incomplete.
A reviewer checking only ACs cannot verify whether Phase 3 (dispatch-by-kind) or 4a (spec derivation) received the intended model-tiering guidance.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: '- [ ] Phase 3 and Phase 4a each contain at least one model-tier reference (e.g. the word "tier" or "strong-tier") — checked by `awk` or `grep` scoped to those sections in SKILL.md.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC2) that verifies Phase 3 dispatch-by-kind includes a seam note identifying cheap-tier-eligible leaf types, and that Phase 4a carries an explicit strong-tier annotation for spec derivation — so all Approach-named amendments are verifiably present.
evidence: Plan Approach: 'targeted amendments to Phases 3, 4a, 4d, plus a new ### 4e-bis escalation ladder'
AC2 only verifies Phase 4d ('Phase 4d states a standard-tier default, a cheap-tier-only-for-transcription rule, and a constraints-in-brief requirement')
AC1 verifies the Model tiering table; AC3 verifies 4e-bis; AC4 verifies no hardcoded size number; AC5 verifies docs-only — none verify Phase 3 or 4a content
Phase 4a already references test-design.md for tier selection; without an explicit strong-tier annotation added here, the orchestrator's tier is ambiguous at the spec-derivation step
findings.finding: The Approach names Phase 3 and 4a amendments as load-bearing parts of the model-tiering mechanism (Phase 3 identifies cheap-tier-eligible leaves at dispatch; Phase 4a anchors the orchestrator's own strong-tier for oracle design), but no Acceptance Criterion verifies either amendment. An implementer who satisfies all five ACs could omit or under-specify these two sections, leaving the mechanism incomplete: without a Phase 3 seam note, the orchestrator has no guidance on which leaf types are eligible for cheap-tier dispatch; without a Phase 4a annotation, the orchestrator may not recognize that it must stay on strong-tier during spec derivation.
findings.location: Approach section ('targeted amendments to Phases 3, 4a, 4d') and Acceptance Criteria (AC1–AC5)
scenarios: Implementer adds Model tiering table + 4d amendment + 4e-bis but leaves Phase 3 dispatch-by-kind and Phase 4a unchanged; all ACs pass but cheap-tier-eligible leaf identification and orchestrator tier at 4a are unspecified.
A future harness executing the skill has no tier signal at Phase 3 to recognize transcription-eligible leaves, so all leaves default to standard-tier regardless of the 4d rule.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2 (or as AC2b): 'Phase 3 dispatch-by-kind names which leaf types are eligible for cheap-tier dispatch — checked by reading the Phase 3 section. Phase 4a carries an explicit strong-tier annotation for spec derivation — checked by reading section 4a.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that verifies the Phase 3 and Phase 4a amendments (e.g., by grepping for a distinctive phrase introduced in each section, or by reading those sections explicitly).
evidence: Approach states: 'targeted amendments to Phases 3, 4a, 4d, plus a new ### 4e-bis escalation ladder'
AC2 says: 'Phase 4d states a standard-tier default…' — only Phase 4d is verified
No AC mentions Phase 3 or Phase 4a content or their verifiable outputs
findings.finding: The Approach explicitly describes 'targeted amendments to Phases 3, 4a, 4d' as deliverables, but Acceptance Criteria AC1–AC5 only verify Phase 4d (AC2), the new Model tiering section (AC1), and 4e-bis (AC3). The Phase 3 and Phase 4a amendments have no covering criterion — an implementer could omit them entirely and still pass all ACs.
findings.location: Approach section ('targeted amendments to Phases 3, 4a, 4d') vs. Acceptance Criteria (AC1–AC5)
scenarios: An implementer skips the Phase 3 and 4a amendments (e.g., the model-tier guidance for planning/scoping phases) and all five ACs still pass — the key orchestration-phase changes are never added.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: '- [ ] Phase 3 contains model-tier guidance for scoping/planning decisions and Phase 4a contains tier-selection criteria — checked by reading those sections in the updated SKILL.md.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence definition of 'transcription work' to the Approach section (or inline in AC2), e.g. 'mechanical line-for-line translation of a fully-specified pseudocode/stub into the target language, requiring no design judgment'.
evidence: AC2 text: 'Phase 4d states a standard-tier default, a cheap-tier-only-for-transcription rule, and a constraints-in-brief requirement'
ABSENCE: neither the Approach section nor the Context section defines 'transcription' as a category of work
findings.finding: The term 'transcription' in AC2 ('a cheap-tier-only-for-transcription rule') is used without definition anywhere in the plan, leaving an executing agent uncertain about which implementation tasks qualify as 'transcription' versus tasks that do not.
scenarios: Two different executors write SKILL.md with incompatible definitions of 'transcription' (e.g. one uses it for all boilerplate; another restricts it to comment/doc rewrites); the AC passes in both cases because no criterion pins the meaning.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, add: 'Transcription work means mechanical conversion of a fully-specified stub or pseudocode to the target language — no architectural judgment required. All other implementation tasks default to standard tier.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'transcription' tasks in the plan body (or in the proposed `## Model tiering` section) — enumerate which subagent work qualifies (e.g., mechanical reformatting, boilerplate generation, literal code translation) and which does not, so the cheap-tier-only rule in 4d has a grounded vocabulary.
evidence: AC2 text: 'Phase 4d states a standard-tier default, a cheap-tier-only-for-transcription rule, and a constraints-in-brief requirement'
grep for 'transcription' across SKILL.md and the plan text: zero matches
The plan body (Approach, Scope) never defines what task types qualify as 'transcription' nor gives examples
An executor implementing 4d must decide which subagent tasks are 'transcription' — without a definition, this decision is arbitrary
findings.finding: The 'cheap-tier-only-for-transcription rule' referenced in AC2 is an undefined decision rule: the word 'transcription' (the task class that triggers cheap-tier use) appears nowhere in the plan body, leaving an executor with no vocabulary to implement the rule.
scenarios: An executor implementing Phase 4d writes the cheap-tier rule but must guess what 'transcription' means — they may apply it to comment generation, code reformatting, or literal transcription tasks, producing different behaviors across executors.
A reviewer checking AC2 cannot verify the rule is correctly scoped without knowing the intended definition of 'transcription'.
findings.suggested_fix: In the `## Model tiering` section, add a row or note defining 'transcription tasks' as a named sub-class of standard-tier work (e.g., 'mechanical tasks with no design judgment: reformatting, boilerplate expansion, literal port of pseudocode to code') and reference this definition in the 4d rule.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the model-weakness escalation triggers in the plan body — specify at minimum: (a) what observable signals indicate model-weakness rather than implementation incompleteness, (b) how many retry cycles precede escalation, and (c) what 'escalation' means mechanically (re-invoke at strong-tier? hand back to orchestrator?).
evidence: AC3 text: 'A `### 4e-bis` escalation ladder exists that distinguishes normal held-out iteration (no escalation) from the model-weakness escalation triggers — checked by reading it'
The plan's Approach section mentions '4e-bis escalation ladder' but gives no trigger conditions, no threshold, no observable signal that separates model-weakness from a normal implementation gap
grep for 'escalat' in SKILL.md: only one match, for 'feature-branch escalation' in 4g, completely unrelated
The processing protocol for 4e-bis is a decision rule an executor must co-locate with the schema — but the rule's inputs and outputs are both absent from the plan
findings.finding: The `### 4e-bis` escalation ladder's decision rules (the 'model-weakness escalation triggers') are never enumerated in the plan body — AC3 only says the section must 'distinguish' normal iteration from those triggers, but what signals constitute model-weakness vs. normal held-out failure is entirely unspecified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC3: '### 4e-bis escalation ladder exists that distinguishes normal held-out iteration (no escalation) from the model-weakness escalation triggers'
scenarios: An executor writing `### 4e-bis` must invent the escalation triggers from scratch — producing rules like 'escalate after N failures' or 'escalate on syntax errors' based on intuition rather than spec.
Two executors implementing this plan will produce different escalation ladders, making the AC unverifiable by 'reading it' since there is no ground truth to read against.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen AC1's proving command to assert both harness names are present in the table, e.g. `grep -n 'Model tiering' SKILL.md && grep -c 'Claude Code' SKILL.md && grep -c 'Codex CLI' SKILL.md` (or scope the row-count grep to the section).
evidence: AC1 text: 'capability-tier table naming both a Claude Code and a Codex CLI example per tier — checked by `grep -n 'Model tiering' && grep -c '^| '`'
grep -c '^| ' counts ALL table rows in the file, not only rows in the Model tiering section, and does not test for harness names
findings.finding: AC1's proving command (`grep -n 'Model tiering' && grep -c '^| '`) does not verify the specific claim that both a Claude Code AND a Codex CLI example appear in the tier table — it only confirms the section heading exists and that some pipe-delimited rows are present (anywhere in the file). An executor who writes the table with only one harness would still pass this check.
scenarios: Executor writes the tier table with only Claude Code examples; grep-c returns a positive count; AC1 is marked green; the Codex CLI mapping is silently absent.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the proving command with: `grep -n 'Model tiering' SKILL.md && grep -c 'Claude Code' SKILL.md && grep -c 'Codex CLI' SKILL.md` — this directly tests the dual-harness claim.

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a disambiguation note in the proposed `## Model tiering` section (and/or in the 4a/4b amendments) explicitly distinguishing 'model tier' (strong/standard/cheap delegation) from 'test tier' (test-design.md §3 granularity), so an executor reading either section cannot confuse the two.
evidence: Approach: 'Frame tiers as *capabilities* (strong / standard / cheap)'
Existing SKILL.md §4a: 'oracle design: the existing-coverage inventory, tier selection'
Existing SKILL.md §4b: 'the tier each mechanism needs'
test-design.md §3 heading: 'Select the test tier' with a table of bug-class→test-shape mappings
Both 'tier' usages will co-exist in the amended SKILL.md with no namespace or disambiguation
findings.finding: The plan introduces a second meaning of 'tier' (model capability tier: strong/standard/cheap) into a document that already uses 'tier' for test tiers (unit/integration/E2E via test-design.md), with no disambiguation instruction — a pink-elephant / instruction-locality collision.
findings.location: Approach section ('Frame tiers as capabilities'); existing SKILL.md §4a ('tier selection') and §4b ('the tier each mechanism needs')
scenarios: An agent reading the amended SKILL.md at Phase 4a sees 'tier selection' and must determine from context whether it refers to test tier (test-design.md §3) or model tier (the new ## Model tiering section) — the same word, in adjacent paragraphs, with different referents.
A harness without strong contextual inference may conflate the two, e.g., applying model-tier logic when selecting test granularity.
findings.suggested_fix: Prefix model-delegation references consistently as 'model tier' and test-granularity references as 'test tier' throughout the amended text, and add one sentence in `## Model tiering`: 'This section uses *model tier* to mean the capability level of the delegated subagent — distinct from *test tier* (unit/integration/E2E) in `test-design.md §3`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one cited reference (harness documentation, changelog, or empirical test) confirming that Claude Code and/or Codex CLI subagents inherit the orchestrator's model tier when no model is explicitly set, so the plan's problem statement is grounded.
evidence: Plan text: 'In both Claude Code and Codex CLI an unset model inherits the orchestrator's (expensive) model, so the subagent silently runs on the strongest tier — the anti-pattern that erases any token saving.'
No citation, link, or empirical evidence is provided for this harness-behavior claim.
The acceptance criteria contain no check that validates this harness behavior.
findings.finding: The plan's core problem statement rests on an unverified behavioral assertion about two third-party harnesses: 'In both Claude Code and Codex CLI an unset model inherits the orchestrator's (expensive) model.' No documentation link, release note, or empirical test is cited. If either harness already enforces a cheaper default for subagents, the problem statement—and thus the shape of the solution—is materially weakened.
findings.location: Context / Problem section: 'In both Claude Code and Codex CLI an unset model inherits the orchestrator's (expensive) model'
scenarios: If Claude Code or Codex CLI already routes subagents to a cheaper default tier, the plan addresses a non-existent problem and may introduce guidance that conflicts with actual harness behavior.
A future reader updating the tier table may set a 'standard-tier default' that actually downgrades from an already-cheap harness default, introducing a regression.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Context/Problem section, add a parenthetical citation: e.g., '(Claude Code: [link to docs/release note]; Codex CLI: [link to config docs])'. If no official documentation exists, note the empirical basis (e.g., 'observed in Claude Code vX.Y') so readers can re-verify as harnesses evolve.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before finalising the `## Model tiering` table, verify that Codex CLI exposes a documented mechanism for selecting per-subagent model tiers (e.g., a `--model` flag or config key), and ground the Codex CLI example entry in that observed mechanism rather than inferred capability.
evidence: Plan text: 'Frame tiers as capabilities (strong / standard / cheap) with Claude Code and Codex CLI example mappings'
AC: 'A ## Model tiering section exists with a capability-tier table naming both a Claude Code and a Codex CLI example per tier'
Current SKILL.md has no Model tiering section — all proposed content is net-new (confirmed by grep)
Evidence class: Unverified — Codex CLI's mechanism for specifying subagent model tiers could not be confirmed from the repo or plan text
findings.finding: The plan asserts that Codex CLI supports capability-tiered model delegation for subagents (i.e., that a Codex CLI orchestrator can explicitly launch a subagent at a cheaper model tier), but provides no evidence that this specific capability surface exists or how it is invoked. The acceptance criterion requires 'a Codex CLI example per tier' in the table, meaning a concrete example will be named in the shipped text. If Codex CLI lacks a user-facing mechanism for selecting per-subagent model tiers, the example entry would be misleading or inoperative when the skill is used under a Codex harness.
findings.location: Approach section ('Claude Code and Codex CLI example mappings') and AC bullet 1 ('naming both a Claude Code and a Codex CLI example per tier')
scenarios: A practitioner running the skill under a Codex CLI harness follows the 'Codex CLI example' in the tier table but the flag/config mechanism doesn't exist or differs from what was documented, causing the cheap-tier delegation to silently fall back to the orchestrator tier — reproducing the exact anti-pattern the plan aims to fix.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief rationale paragraph to the `## Model tiering` section in SKILL.md explaining *why* capability labels are used instead of product names (cross-harness portability, model-name drift), so the decision is self-documenting after merge.
evidence: Plan Approach: 'Frame tiers as *capabilities* (strong / standard / cheap) with Claude Code and Codex CLI example mappings, rather than hardcoding product names — the skill is cross-harness (AGENTS.md-class prose read by non-Claude harnesses) and model names drift across releases.'
AC item 1 only checks for the existence of a table (grep for 'Model tiering' and count table rows); it does not require an explanatory rationale paragraph.

The rejected-alternative reasoning ('Rejected alternative: naming Opus/Sonnet/Haiku directly — simpler but breaks portability to Codex and rots as models are renamed') appears only in the PR plan, not in any committed artifact.
findings.finding: The plan does not confirm that the committed `## Model tiering` section will carry the design rationale for capability-based naming (cross-harness portability, model-name drift) rather than leaving that rationale only in the PR description. After merge, the 'why' behind the tier-as-capability framing will be invisible to future editors of SKILL.md unless it is embedded in the section itself.
findings.location: Approach section / AC item 1 (the `## Model tiering` section spec)
scenarios: A future contributor edits SKILL.md and, seeing no inline rationale, 'simplifies' the tiers back to product names, re-introducing the portability problem the plan explicitly solved.
A harness maintainer reading only SKILL.md cannot tell why capability labels were preferred over concrete model names.
findings.suggested_fix: Prepend 2–3 sentences to the `## Model tiering` section, e.g.: 'Tiers are expressed as capabilities (strong / standard / cheap) rather than product names. This keeps the skill portable across harnesses (Claude Code, Codex CLI, others) and stable as model names are renamed or superseded. See the table below for current example mappings.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify at least one concrete capability descriptor per tier per harness in the plan body (e.g., for Claude Code: strong = 'claude-opus-*', standard = 'claude-sonnet-*', cheap = 'claude-haiku-*'; for Codex CLI: analogous o-series mapping), so the tier table's value vocabulary is defined before implementation.
evidence: AC1: 'A `## Model tiering` section exists with a capability-tier table naming both a Claude Code and a Codex CLI example per tier'
Approach: 'Frame tiers as *capabilities* (strong / standard / cheap) with Claude Code and Codex CLI example mappings'
Approach: 'Rejected alternative: naming Opus/Sonnet/Haiku directly' — implies concrete names exist for the accepted approach but does not state them
No plan section enumerates the actual model identifiers or capability descriptors the table will contain
An executor must invent the table's cell values, which are the examples an implementer of 4d will use to configure subagents
findings.finding: The capability-tier table's concrete harness-example values (which specific Claude Code and Codex CLI model identifiers map to strong/standard/cheap) are described in the Approach as being included but are never specified in the plan — leaving the value vocabulary of a referenced enum undefined.
findings.location: Approach section: 'with Claude Code and Codex CLI example mappings'; AC1: 'a capability-tier table naming both a Claude Code and a Codex CLI example per tier'
scenarios: An executor writing the tier table inserts currently-available model names that may already be stale at review time, or picks examples that don't reflect the 'capability not product-name' framing the plan intends.
Two executors produce different tables with different examples, making AC1's grep-based check pass for both even though they encode different harness mappings.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
